stimulus:
Percentage of Beekeepers in Three Provinces of Hidalgo Who Are Indigenous, by Honey Type Produced

Provincewildflower honeyavocado honeymesquite honey
north Hidalgo32.1%17.4%61.2%
central Hidalgo38.2%21.7%46.5%
south Hidalgo34.6%19.1%54.8%

Anthropologist Mariana Vargas and her colleagues surveyed small-scale beekeepers in three provinces of Hidalgo, Mexico—which features a forested north, a populated center, and an arid southern plain—to study their honey production. In several provinces, Indigenous beekeepers were notably prominent in producing specific honey varieties, sometimes accounting for a majority of those producing such varieties; for example, ______
stem: Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to complete the example?

answerOptions.content: Indigenous beekeepers represented a relatively similar share of wildflower honey producers across the three provinces of Hidalgo.
most of the mesquite honey producers in north and south Hidalgo were Indigenous beekeepers.
more Indigenous beekeepers in central Hidalgo produced avocado honey than produced wildflower honey.
most of the wildflower honey producers and most of the mesquite honey producers in south Hidalgo were Indigenous beekeepers.

rationale:
Choice B is correct. The table shows that of mesquite honey producers, about 61.2% in north Hidalgo and about 54.8% in south Hidalgo are Indigenous—both majorities. That directly supports the claim that Indigenous beekeepers can constitute a majority of producers of specific honey varieties in certain provinces.

Choice A is incorrect: although wildflower percentages are similar across provinces (32.1%, 38.2%, 34.6%), none exceeds 50%, so this doesn't illustrate a majority. Choice C is incorrect: in central Hidalgo, more Indigenous beekeepers produce wildflower honey (38.2%) than avocado honey (21.7%)—the comparison is reversed. Choice D is incorrect: in south Hidalgo only 34.6% of wildflower producers are Indigenous, not a majority.
